title: The Silk Road
description: Test your knowledge on the history of the Silk Road!

choices.content: The name of a single trade route that carried goods and ideas across Eurasia
A name for several trade routes that carried goods and ideas across Eurasia
A name for several trade routes that carried just silk across Eurasia

hints: Pax Romana does not mean "The Roman Pact."

choices.content: The Roman Place, referring to the extent of the empire
The Roman Pact, referring to a peace treaty Rome made with China
The Roman Peace, referring to an era relatively free of war in the empire

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did the Han Dynasty under Emperor Wu desire more and better horses?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Emperor Wu's wish for more horses was not connected to agriculture.

choices.content: To fight the Xiongnu and other enemies more effectively
To better facilitate long-distance trade across the Silk Road
To facilitate agriculture by using the horses to plow fields efficiently

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the benefit to sailors who understood the patterns of monsoon winds in the Indian Ocean?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Sailors did not expect to gain wealth by reporting the timing of the monsoon winds.

choices.content: There was no benefit to knowing since the winds were always the same
Emperors would pay them for this information
It allowed them to time their travel between ports in the Indian Ocean and the Red Sea

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was exchanged along the Silk Road?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Trade goods only
Ideas and diseases only
Trade goods, ideas, and diseases.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read this quote below and answer the question that follows: 

> “I can see clothes of silk, if materials that do not hide the body, nor even one's decency, can be called clothes. ... Wretched flocks of maids labor so that the adulteress may be visible through her thin dress, so that her husband has no more acquaintance than any outsider or foreigner with his wife's body.”

From: Seneca the Younger, _Declamations_ Vol. I.

**What can Seneca's views tell us about the effects of the silk trade on Rome?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Not all Romans were happy about the silk trade
Romans were traveling to China to buy silk
All Romans saw the silk trade as an entirely good thing
Most Romans felt that silk should not be used to make clothing

assessment_items.question_content:
Read this quote below and answer the question that follows: 

> "It is worthy to be observed, that there is not a year but it costs our state [Rome] to furnish into India, 500,000 sesterces [Roman unit of currency]. For which the Indians send back merchandise, which at Rome is sold for a hundred times as much as it cost."

From: Pliny the Elder, _Natural History_, Book VII.

**What does this quote from Pliny imply about the reasons that Romans traded with India?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Goods from India were worth the same amount in both India and Rome
Goods that could be purchased cheaply from India were very valuable in Rome
Romans were probably trading for things in India that were also available in Rome
Indians wanted Roman-made goods

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question that follows: 

>The Silk Road...is often presented as a link between two continents of civilization—the west and the east—and the area between them as an ocean of emptiness. But routes depend on civilizations to maintain them, ensure security and provide the infrastructure for rest stops, inns, water and other requirements of the traveller. The Silk Road could only emerge when there were stable regimes [governments] along its length, producing enough wealth to create surplus goods and a demand for items beyond the necessities of daily life. 

From: British Library, _The Silk Road: Trade, Travel, War and Faith_ (Chicago: Serindia Publications, 2004), 13. 
 
**According to the author, which of the following was a _cause_ of the emergence of Silk Road trade routes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The establishment of stable governments along the routes
The creation of rest stops and inns
The necessity of the goods being traded
The long distance between Rome and Han China

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question that follows: 

>The Silk Road...is often presented as a link between two continents of civilization—the west and the east—and the area between them as an ocean of emptiness. But routes depend on civilizations to maintain them, ensure security and provide the infrastructure for rest stops, inns, water and other requirements of the traveller. The Silk Road could only emerge when there were stable regimes [governments] along its length, producing enough wealth to create surplus goods and a demand for items beyond the necessities of daily life. 

From: British Library, _The Silk Road: Trade, Travel, War and Faith_ (Chicago: Serindia Publications, 2004), 13.

**Which of the following claims is the author making in this paragraph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Silk Road connected to civilizations with very little in between them
The Silk Road was only possible because there were civilizations along most of its length
The Silk Road would have existed with or without civilizations along its length
The Silk Road caused the rise of Han China and Rome

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage below and answer the question that follows: 

A number of important changes were taking place in Khotan [a kingdom along the Silk Road trade routes] in the first century [CE]. Among these, we know that between 30-150 [CE] the kings of Khotan issued their own coins. These were the bilingual [in two languages]...with an inscription on one side in the Kharaththi [a central Asian/northern Indian] script, and a Chinese inscription on the other side… The kings of Khotan intended that these coins should closely reflect the coins of the early Kushan rulers of Bactria and northwest India… The bilingual elements and the comparative weights suggests that the … coins were designed to fit both systems.  
 
From: British Library, _The Silk Road: Trade, Travel, War and Faith_ (Chicago: Serindia Publications, 2004), 27.
 
**What is the _best_ evidence for the claim that the Kingdom of Khotan was trading with both northern India and China?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Khotan issued bilingual coins with the languages of both regions
Khotan was geographically between China and India
Khotan existed at the same time as Han China
Khotan was experiencing changes when the coins were issued
